steps.title: What'll You Have? ☕
steps.desc: The Dutch love their coffee breaks! Let's learn to order drinks — the first step to surviving in a Dutch café.

steps.note: A de-word! The Dutch drink an average of 3-4 cups of coffee per day — the most in Europe!
A de-word. 'Graag' means 'gladly/preferably'. 'Ik drink graag...' = I like to drink...

steps.exampleEn: May I have a glass of water?
steps.note: A het-word! 'Een glas water' = a glass of water. Very similar to English.
A het-word! The diminutive 'biertje' is super common — it sounds friendlier. Heineken is Dutch!
A de-word. 'Rode wijn' = red wine, 'witte wijn' = white wine. Sounds like English 'wine'.
A de-word. The Dutch drink a lot of milk — karnemelk (buttermilk) is uniquely popular!

steps.title: 🗣️ How to order
steps.text:
At a Dutch café or restaurant:

☕ Mag ik een koffie? — May I have a coffee?
🍺 Een biertje, alsjeblieft. — A beer, please.
💧 Doe maar een water. — Just give me a water.
🍵 Ik wil graag thee. — I'd like tea.

'Doe maar...' is a very Dutch phrase meaning 'just give me...' or 'I'll have...' — super casual and commonly used!

deepDive.title: The Dutch coffee culture — 'koffietijd'
deepDive.text:
Coffee isn't just a drink in the Netherlands — it's a social institution.

'Koffie?' is the first thing you'll hear when visiting any Dutch home or office. Refusing coffee can even be considered slightly rude!

Common coffee terms:
• Koffie verkeerd — literally 'wrong coffee' = coffee with lots of milk (like a latte)
• Espresso — same as everywhere
• Cappuccino — also common
• Bakkie — slang for a cup of coffee

Many Dutch workplaces have a daily 'koffiepauze' (coffee break) that's almost sacred.

steps.hint: Think about what category water falls into — liquid, substance...

steps.q: How would you casually order a beer?

steps.title: Eet smakelijk! 🍞
steps.desc: 'Eet smakelijk' means 'enjoy your meal'! Let's learn essential food words — many are key to understanding Dutch menus.
goals: De vs het with food nouns

steps.title: De vs Het — Why It Matters
steps.text:
Food nouns are a great way to practice articles:

🔴 de-words (coral): de kaas, de vis, de groente, de aardappel
🟣 het-words (purple): het brood, het ei, het vlees

Notice the teach cards are color-coded!
Coral background = de word
Purple background = het word

There's no logic — just memorize each noun with its article.

Full reference → Grammar tab → A1 → Articles: De vs Het

steps.example: Wij eten brood bij het ontbijt.
steps.note: A het-word! Bread is THE Dutch food. The Dutch eat bread for lunch every single day — it's called 'boterham'.
A de-word. The Netherlands is one of the world's biggest cheese exporters — Gouda, Edam, Leiden cheese!

steps.exampleEn: Do you want a boiled egg?
steps.note: A het-word! Plural: de eieren (the eggs). 'Een gebakken ei' = a fried egg.
A het-word! 'Geen' = no/not any. 'Ik eet geen vlees' is useful if you're vegetarian.
A de-word! The Dutch love haring (herring) — eaten raw with onions! A must-try street food.
A de-word. The classic Dutch dinner: meat + potatoes + groente. Called 'AVG' (aardappelen, vlees, groente).
A de-word. Literally 'earth-apple'! Plural: aardappelen. Stamppot (mashed potatoes + veggies) is the national comfort food.
THE most Dutch word! Used for food, weather, feelings — anything positive. 'Lekker weer' = nice weather, 'lekker slapen' = sleep well.

steps.title: 😋 'Lekker' — the ultimate Dutch word
steps.text:
'Lekker' is everywhere in Dutch:

🍽️ Het eten is lekker! — The food is tasty!
☀️ Lekker weer vandaag! — Nice weather today!
😴 Slaap lekker! — Sleep well!
🏖️ Lekker rustig. — Nicely quiet.
💆 Ik voel me lekker. — I feel good.

If you only learn one Dutch adjective, make it 'lekker'!

steps.hint: You learned this one at breakfast!
The most positive word in Dutch!
The most Dutch compliment for food

steps.title: At the Restaurant 🍽️
steps.desc: Time to put it all together and order a full meal in Dutch! You'll learn key restaurant phrases.

steps.note: A het-word! Same as French. You'll also hear 'de kaart' (the card) meaning the menu.
A de-word. In the Netherlands, you usually have to ASK for the bill — they won't bring it automatically.
A het-word. Dutch breakfast = bread with cheese, ham, hagelslag (chocolate sprinkles!), peanut butter, jam.

steps.example: Wat eet je voor de lunch?
steps.exampleEn: What do you eat for lunch?
steps.note: A de-word. Dutch lunch is simple: broodjes (sandwiches). The warm meal is dinner.

steps.example: Het avondeten is om zes uur.
steps.note: A het-word. Literally 'evening-eating'. Dutch people eat dinner early — often around 6 PM!

steps.title: 🍽️ Restaurant survival phrases
steps.text:
Essential phrases:

📋 Mag ik het menu? — May I see the menu?
🤔 Wat is de soep van de dag? — What is the soup of the day?
☕ Ik wil graag een koffie. — I'd like a coffee.
🍽️ Ik wil graag bestellen. — I'd like to order.
🧾 Mag ik de rekening? — May I have the bill?
💳 Kan ik pinnen? — Can I pay by card?

'Pinnen' = paying by debit card (very Dutch — cash is rare!)

steps.q: How do you ask for the bill?

steps.hint: What do you look at to choose your food?
Pinnen = to pay by debit card
